message.reasoning_content:
We are given a document about self-consistency for chain-of-thought reasoning. We need to assess each criterion.

First, note: title and abstract only. The document has title T and abstract sentences S1-S5.

Criterion I1: "The article describes or proposes at least one concrete prompt-engineering operation: a way to construct, structure, sequence, transform, verify, or constrain prompts or model outputs. Merely using an LLM, evaluating an LLM, or naming a prompting family does not by itself satisfy this criterion."

The article proposes "self-consistency", which is a decoding strategy. It involves sampling multiple reasoning paths and then selecting the most consistent answer. This is a method applied after the prompt. According to the definitions, "prompt-engineering operation" includes "constrain prompts or model outputs". Self-consistency constrains how the output is selected from multiple samples. It is not just using an LLM or naming a prompting family; it describes a specific operation (sampling and marginalizing). So I1 is met.
Evidence: S2 "we propose a new decoding strategy, self-consistency, to replace the naive greedy decoding" and S3 describes the operation.

Criterion I2: "The title or abstract exposes an identifiable prompt structure, instruction, sequence, procedure, or transferable method that a reader could reproduce or operationalize. Reporting only comparative performance of named prompting methods, without describing a transferable technique or structure, does not satisfy this criterion."

The article describes self-consistency as a transferable method. It explains the procedure: sample diverse reasoning paths, then select most consistent answer. This is reproducible. So I2 is met.
Evidence: S2 and S3.

Criterion I3: "The technique targets large language models or agents whose behavior is based on large language models."

The abstract mentions "pre-trained large language models" in S1. So yes, it targets LLMs. I3 is met.
Evidence: S1 "pre-trained large language models".

Criterion I4: "The technique is applicable by a practitioner through prompts, instructions, context, inference-time orchestration, or output constraints, without requiring the practitioner to train or fine-tune model weights or learned prompt parameters."

Self-consistency is a decoding strategy, applied at inference time. It does not require training or fine-tuning. It is applicable by a practitioner using prompts (chain-of-thought) and then applying the self-consistency method on outputs. So I4 is met.
Evidence: S2, S3 describe decoding strategy, no mention of training.

Now exclusion criteria.

E1: "The article discusses LLMs or agents but does not propose or describe an actionable prompt technique."

Since we already found that it describes an actionable prompt technique (self-consistency), this exclusion is not met. Actually, we need to check: does it propose or describe an actionable prompt technique? Yes, so E1 should be "not_met" with evidence that contradicts the exclusion (i.e., evidence that it does propose a technique). But note: E1 is an exclusion criterion meaning we exclude if it is true. So if the article does propose an actionable technique, E1 is false (not_met). So status=not_met. Evidence: S2, S3.

E2: "The article is solely about model architecture, training, fine-tuning, model weights, or learned prompt parameters, without a practitioner-usable prompting component."

The article is about a decoding strategy, not about training or fine-tuning. It uses pre-trained models. So it is not solely about model training. Thus E2 is not_met. Evidence: S1 mentions "pre-trained large language models", but the focus is on decoding. So not_met.

E3: "The article is a benchmark or model evaluation without a transferable prompt method, prompt structure, or prompting procedure."

The article does evaluate on benchmarks, but it also proposes a transferable method (self-consistency). So it is not just a benchmark. E3 is not_met. Evidence: S2 proposes self-consistency.

E4: "The article reports a domain application of an LLM without describing the prompt method in actionable detail."

This is not a domain application; it is a method paper. It describes the method in actionable detail. So E4 is not_met. Evidence: The whole abstract describes the method.

E5: "The article is an opinion, editorial, or survey that does not expose at least one isolable and actionable prompt technique."

It is a research paper presenting a new method, not an opinion or survey. So E5 is not_met. Evidence: S2 "we propose a new decoding strategy".

Thus all exclusion criteria are not_met.

Now we need to output JSON with all criteria.
